children.value:
A function can be convex on one interval and concave on another, as we saw in
section 7.3 for the roller coaster height profile. The point where the curve
transitions from one type to the other marks a qualitative change in shape:
the bending reverses direction. On the lift hill, this transition occurs at

children.html: x=30 mx = 30~\text{m}x=30 m

children.value:
, where the track changes from curving upward like the base
of a bowl to curving downward like the crown of a dome. Points of this kind
appear throughout engineering, from the bending moment diagrams of loaded beams
to the transition curves of roads and railways, and identifying them precisely
requires a careful argument.
You know the definition of an
as a point where the convexity of the function changes sign.
You can identify candidates for inflection points using the necessary condition

children.html: f′′(x0)=0f''(x_0) = 0f′′(x0​)=0
f′′(x0)=0f''(x_0) = 0f′′(x0​)=0

children.value: is necessary but not sufficient for an inflection point, and you can verify the

and concave just after. The curvature does not merely reach
zero and bounce back; it crosses through zero and reverses sign. This crossing
is what defines an inflection point.

not come with a sign change is not an inflection point.
What does the inflection point look like on the graph?

children.label: What does the inflection point look like on the graph?

children.html: x=30x = 30x=30

children.value:
is visible in the geometry of the lift hill profile.
On the lower, convex half the tangent line at any point lies below the curve
nearby: the curve climbs above its own linear approximation. On the upper,
concave half the tangent line lies above the curve: the curve falls short of
its linear approximation. At the inflection point itself the tangent line
crosses from one side of the curve to the other, momentarily coinciding in
both value and slope with the track but transitioning from an underestimate to
an overestimate.

This crossing is one of the sharpest visual signatures of an inflection point.
For the marble track studied in chapters 5 and 6, no such crossing occurs
because

children.html: f′′(x)=0.04>0f''(x) = 0.04 > 0f′′(x)=0.04>0

children.value:
everywhere: the parabola is convex on its entire
domain, and its tangent lines never cross the curve.

The location of the inflection point is also physically significant for the
passengers. On the lower half, the track pushes upward against the car with a
centripetal force that adds to the apparent weight, pressing passengers into
their seats. On the upper half, the track curves in the opposite sense, and
the centripetal force direction reverses, reducing the apparent weight. The
inflection point at

children.html: x=30x = 30x=30

children.value:
m marks the boundary between these two
experiences.

Where do inflection points appear in engineering?

children.label: Where do inflection points appear in engineering?

children.value:
Inflection points arise wherever a quantity transitions between two opposite
curvature regimes, and they serve as natural reference points in design and
analysis.
In beam theory, the deflection curve

children.html: y(x)y(x)y(x)
EI y′′=M(x)EI\,y'' = M(x)EIy′′=M(x)
M(x)M(x)M(x)
M(x)=0M(x) = 0M(x)=0

children.value:
, the second derivative
of the deflection vanishes, and if

children.html: MMM

children.value:
changes sign there, the deflection
curve has an inflection point. Engineers call this position the

. It separates the region where the beam sags (convex upward)
from the region where it hogs (concave), and it determines where to place
construction joints and how to arrange reinforcement in concrete structures.

In road and railway design, every transition from a sag curve to a crest curve
introduces an inflection point in the vertical profile. The curvature is zero
at that point, and design standards specify the minimum length of the
transition zone around it to ensure that the change in centripetal force
imposed on vehicles remains within comfortable bounds. The mathematics behind
those standards is exactly the analysis of higher-order derivatives that we
have developed in this chapter.

In the analysis of control systems, a sigmoid-shaped step response has exactly
one inflection point, corresponding to the moment of maximum rate of change in
the output. Identifying this point, and measuring the slope of the response
curve there, is a standard method for estimating the time constants of the
underlying system model.

We have defined the inflection point as a point where the convexity of a
function changes, identified the necessary condition

children.html: f′′(x0)=0f''(x_0) = 0f′′(x0​)=0

children.value:
, and shown
through the example of

children.html: g(x)=x4g(x) = x^4g(x)=x4

children.value:
that this condition is not sufficient on
its own. For the roller coaster height profile

children.html: h(x)h(x)h(x)
x=30 mx = 30~\text{m}x=30 m

children.value:
marks the transition from the bowl-shaped lower half of
the lift hill to the dome-shaped upper half, at the point of maximum gradient,
mid-height on the rise.

This chapter has built three levels of analysis on top of the derivative
function introduced in chapter 5. The second derivative measures the rate of
change of the slope. Its sign classifies every interval as convex or concave.
A zero of the second derivative that comes with a sign change marks an
inflection point, a boundary between the two regimes. The third derivative,
jerk, measures how quickly the acceleration changes and is the physical
quantity that determines passenger comfort and structural impulse loading.

These tools are the direct preparation for the analysis of extrema in the next
chapter. We will ask not just whether a function is increasing or decreasing,
but whether a stationary point, a point where

children.html: f′(x0)=0f'(x_0) = 0f′(x0​)=0

children.value:
, is a local
minimum, a local maximum, or neither. The answer comes from the sign of the
second derivative at that point, in what is called the second derivative test.
The notions of convexity and concavity developed here are exactly what that
test requires, and we will find that the roller coaster profile provides a
natural entry point for that discussion as well.
